summary:
Manoj is a 23-year-old recent graduate from Nepal who has just transitioned from university life to full-time work at an accounting firm in London. He presents as highly self-aware and disciplined, actively managing his habits around both social media consumption and food ordering. His relationship with food delivery has undergone a dramatic transformation - during university, he was a heavy user of Uber Eats with a student subscription, but gained significant weight (from 71kg to 80-85kg) which prompted a complete lifestyle overhaul. Now living alone and cooking daily, he has deliberately cancelled his subscription to avoid the temptation of frequent ordering, viewing this as a form of self-control rather than financial constraint.

Manoj demonstrates remarkable intentionality in his daily routines - he meal preps salads for work, maintains strict eating windows (no food after 8-9pm), and has deleted TikTok to manage screen time. His cooking serves dual emotional functions: healthy meals feel stressful and obligatory, while making traditional Nepalese momos provides genuine stress relief and cultural connection. He actively avoids food content on social media because it triggers ordering impulses, showing sophisticated awareness of his own behavioral patterns. Despite his current restraint, he remains loyal to Uber Eats over competitors due to a single negative experience with Deliveroo's slower delivery time, and he's pragmatic about potentially returning to subscriptions once he reaches his health goals.
personality_notes: Self-disciplined and methodical communicator who gives thoughtful, detailed responses. Shows high self-awareness about his habits and triggers. Speaks with quiet determination about lifestyle changes, not defensive but matter-of-fact about past behaviors.
food_relationship: Food delivery represents a battle between convenience and health goals - he's currently winning through deliberate restriction and home cooking, but acknowledges it as an ongoing management challenge rather than a resolved issue.

key_moments.description: Reveals significant weight gain during university (71kg to 80-85kg) from frequent food delivery, leading to complete lifestyle change
key_moments.significance: Core motivation behind current restrictive approach to delivery apps - health concerns trump convenience

key_moments.description: Explains actively blocking food content on Instagram because it triggers late-night ordering
key_moments.significance: Shows sophisticated understanding of his own behavioral triggers and proactive management strategies

key_moments.description: Describes comparative test with friend where Deliveroo took 30-35 minutes vs Uber's 10-15 minutes for same restaurant
key_moments.significance: Single negative experience that permanently shifted platform loyalty - demonstrates how service failures can have lasting impact

what_not_said: Details about his girlfriend's influence on food choices
Specific financial pressures despite being a new graduate
Social dynamics with friends around food ordering
initial_hypotheses: Health consciousness is driving current behavior more than financial constraints - he can afford delivery but chooses restriction
Cultural food connection (Nepalese cuisine) provides emotional regulation that Western fast food cannot
His systematic approach to habit change suggests he may return to delivery apps once health goals are met, but with better boundaries
questions_for_coding: How do other participants manage the tension between convenience and health goals?
What role does cultural food play in stress management vs. delivery food?
How common is deliberate subscription cancellation as a self-control strategy?
Do other participants show similar awareness of social media food content as a trigger?
